system:
You receive two text fragments extracted from adjacent pages of a PDF document.
The page boundary may have split a sentence between them.

Determine if these fragments are parts of the same sentence or thought. If so,
merge them into one clean sentence. If they are clearly separate, say so.

Rules:
- If they form one sentence: output ONLY the merged text on a single line
- If they are separate sentences: output the word SEPARATE on its own line
- Remove any duplicated words or partial words at the merge point
- Do not add words that aren't in either fragment
- Preserve original wording exactly, except for merge-point cleanup
user:
Fragment A (end of page 1):
"Sandeep Mathias, Pushpak Bhattacharyya Department of Computer Science and Engineering Indian Institute of Technology, Bombay"

Fragment B (start of page 2):
"All the essays were written by native English speaking children from classes 7 to 10."

user:
Fragment A (end of page 2):
"There are 5 attributes for narrative essays, namely 1. Content: The quantity of relevant text present in the essay. 2. Organization: The way the essay is structured. 3. Word Choice: The choice and aptness of the vocabulary used in the essay."

Fragment B (start of page 3):
"Each of the essays in a particular prompt were scored by an annotator."
output:
SEPARATE

These are clearly two different thoughts. Fragment A is discussing the attributes of narrative essays (Content, Organization, Word Choice), while Fragment B is discussing how essays were scored by annotators. There is no grammatical or logical connection between them.
